perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of India.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of India, excluding any laws relating to intellectual property rights.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding laws relating to intellectual property rights, the contract attempts to bypass statutory protections afforded to creators under Indian law, such as the Copyright Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957 defines the works in which copyright subsists, including original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works. Excluding intellectual property law from the governing law of the contract directly contradicts the statutory protections afforded to copyright holders under this Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: India Code: Invalid URL or Argument(s). Invalid URL or Argument(s). The system has experienced an error. Please try to do what you were doing again, and if the problem persists, please contact us so we can fix the problem. Go to the DSpace home page
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes intellectual property laws from governing the agreement, which directly contradicts the protections afforded under Indian law, such as the Copyright Act. The Perturbation Explanation notes the bypass of statutory protections, and the Law Explanation specifies the contradiction with Section 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957. The original contract states that the laws of India shall govern the agreement. The changed text, however, attempts to remove a specific subset of Indian law, which creates a legal loophole and undermines the statutory protections intended by Indian intellectual property law. Even though the scraped snippets are unavailable, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation clearly outline the contradiction, and the change directly contradicts the application of Indian law.

perturbation.original_text: 14. The Parties hereto undertake that any dispute which may arise between them shall first be dealt with in the manner stated below, irrespective of the other recourse, which any Party may have in law or in equity. 14.1 If any dispute arises between the Parties hereto during the subsistence of this Agreement or thereafter, in connection with the validity, interpretation, implementation or alleged material breach of any provision of this Agreement or regarding a question, including the questions as to whether the termination of this Agreement by one Party hereto has been legitimate, both Parties hereto shall endeavour to settle such dispute amicably. If the Parties fail to bring about an amicable settlement within a period of thirty (30) days, either Party to the dispute may give ten (10) days notice of invocation of dispute settlement by the Film Writers Association, Mumbai, to the other Party in writing.The Parties hereto shall submit to such mediation award by the Film Writers Association and the award shall be enforceable in any competent court of law in Mumbai.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. The Parties hereto undertake that any dispute which may arise between them shall first be dealt with in the manner stated below, irrespective of the other recourse, which any Party may have in law or in equity. 14.1 If any dispute arises between the Parties hereto during the subsistence of this Agreement or thereafter, in connection with the validity, interpretation, implementation or alleged material breach of any provision of this Agreement or regarding a question, including the questions as to whether the termination of this Agreement by one Party hereto has been legitimate, both Parties hereto shall endeavour to settle such dispute amicably. If the Parties fail to bring about an amicable settlement within a period of thirty (30) days, either Party to the dispute may give ten (10) days notice of invocation of dispute settlement by the Film Writers Association, Mumbai, to the other Party in writing. The Parties hereto *may* submit to such mediation award by the Film Writers Association and the award *may* be enforceable in any competent court of law in Mumbai.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' weakens the enforceability of the mediation award, potentially contradicting provisions for dispute resolution under Indian law, particularly the Arbitration and Conciliation Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 35 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 states that an arbitral award shall be recognized as binding and, upon application in writing to the competent court, be enforced. Changing the language to 'may' contradicts the binding nature and enforceability prescribed by this law.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' significantly weakens the enforceability of the mediation award. The original text mandates submission to and enforcement of the award, while the modified text makes it optional. The Law Explanation references Section 35 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, stating that an arbitral award 'shall be recognized as binding and...be enforced.' By changing 'shall' to 'may,' the modified contract directly contradicts this binding nature and enforceability, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. The Receiving party acknowledges that damages are not a sufficient remedy for the Disclosing Party for any breach of any of the Receiving Party’s undertakings herein provided and the Receiving Party further acknowledges that the Disclosing party is entitled to specific performance or injunctive relief (as appropriate) as a remedy for any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or any undertakings hereunder by the Receiving Party, in addition to and without prejudice any other remedies available to the Disclosing Party in law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. The Receiving party acknowledges that damages may be the sole remedy for the Disclosing Party for any breach of any of the Receiving Party’s undertakings herein provided.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that damages 'may be the sole remedy,' the clause eliminates the Disclosing Party's right to seek specific performance or injunctive relief, potentially contradicting the Specific Relief Act, 1963, which provides for such remedies in certain cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 states that specific performance of a contract may be enforced when there exists no standard for ascertaining the actual damage caused by the non-performance of the act agreed to be done. This modified clause may unduly restrict remedies available under the Act.

perturbation.justification: The changed text states that damages 'may be the sole remedy,' which directly contradicts the Law Explanation that cites Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, which states that specific performance may be enforced when damages are not easily ascertainable. The original text acknowledges specific performance or injunctive relief. The modification removes the Disclosing Party's right to seek specific performance, creating a legal loophole and contradiction, aligning with the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The Receiving Party shall hold and keep in strictest confidence any and all Confidential Information and shall treat the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care and protection as it would treat its own Confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Receiving Party shall hold and keep in confidence any and all Confidential Information and shall treat the Confidential Information with a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the obligation to 'strictest confidence' and replacing 'at least the same degree of care and protection as it would treat its own Confidential information' with 'reasonable degree of care' reduces the standard of care. This could be problematic if the 'reasonable degree of care' falls below what is expected under Indian law for protecting trade secrets and confidential information, potentially violating obligations under common law principles of confidentiality and equity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although India lacks a specific statute governing trade secrets, courts rely on common law principles of equity and confidentiality. These principles generally require a higher standard of care than simply 'reasonable care' when protecting confidential information, especially when a duty of confidence exists. Reducing the standard could be interpreted as a failure to adequately protect trade secrets, leading to potential liability.

portal v t e The tort of breach of confidence is, in United Kingdom law and the United States law , a common-law tort that protects private information conveyed in confidence . [ 1 ] A claim for breach of confidence typically requires the information to be of a confidential nature, which was communicated in confidence and was disclosed to the detriment of the claimant. Establishing a breach of confidentiality depends on proving the existence and breach of a duty of confidentiality. Courts in the United States look at the nature of the relationship between the parties. Most commonly, breach of confidentiality applies to the patient-physician relationship , [ 2 ] but it can also apply to relationships involving banks, hospitals, insurance companies, and many others. [ 3 ] There was no clear tort of breach of confidence in other common-law jurisdictions such as the United Kingdom or Australia ; however, there is an equitable doctrine of breach of confidence . In OBG Ltd v Allan [2007] UKHL 21 the Law Lords clarified that Economic torts in English law do exist. See also [ edit ] Abuse of information Misuse of private information Breach of confidence in English law United States free speech exceptions References [ edit ] ^ "Breach of confidence" . ^ Networks, Institute of Medicine (US) Committee on Regional Health Data; Donaldson, Molla S.; Lohr, Kathleen N. (1994). Confidentiality and Privacy of Personal Data . National Academies Press (US).
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'strictest confidence' and 'at least the same degree of care' as the Receiving Party uses for its own confidential information. The changed text reduces this to simply 'confidence' and a 'reasonable degree of care.' The Law Explanation states that common law principles of equity and confidentiality, which are the basis for trade secret protection in India, generally require a higher standard of care than 'reasonable care.' The scraped snippet from Wikipedia describes breach of confidence as a tort that protects private information conveyed in confidence, requiring the information to be of a confidential nature, communicated in confidence, and disclosed to the detriment of the claimant. By reducing the standard of care, the changed text increases the risk of a breach of confidence claim being successful, because it weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This creates a legal contradiction as it potentially undermines the protection expected under common law principles, making the contract more vulnerable to legal challenges.
